expanded_description: Markovnikov's Rule is a fundamental principle in organic chemistry that predicts the regioselectivity of electrophilic addition reactions to unsymmetrical alkenes. This rule states that when a protic acid (HX) adds to an asymmetric alkene, the hydrogen attaches to the carbon with more hydrogen substituents, while the halide attaches to the carbon with fewer hydrogen substituents (more substituted carbon). Understanding this rule is essential for predicting product structures in alkene addition reactions and forms the foundation for understanding carbocation stability and reaction mechanisms.

core_concept.principle: The Rich Get Richer: More Substituted Carbons Stabilize Positive Charge Better
core_concept.explanation: Markovnikov's Rule arises from the relative stability of carbocation intermediates formed during electrophilic addition. When HX adds to an unsymmetrical alkene, two possible carbocations can form. The more stable carbocation (tertiary > secondary > primary) forms preferentially because it has lower activation energy. The halide then attacks this more stable carbocation, resulting in the halogen attaching to the more substituted carbon. This stability order is due to hyperconjugation and inductive effects from alkyl groups that donate electron density to stabilize the electron-deficient carbocation center.

image.alt: Diagram showing Markovnikov addition to propene with carbocation intermediates
image.caption: Markovnikov addition: H adds to carbon with more H's; X adds to more substituted carbon via more stable carbocation intermediate.

sections.introduction: Markovnikov's Rule, formulated by Russian chemist Vladimir Markovnikov in 1869, is one of the most important predictive rules in organic chemistry. It allows chemists to determine which of two possible products will be the major product when a protic acid adds to an unsymmetrical alkene. This rule is not arbitrary—it reflects fundamental principles of carbocation stability and reaction kinetics. Understanding Markovnikov's Rule is essential for predicting reaction outcomes, designing synthetic routes, and understanding the mechanistic basis of electrophilic addition reactions.

key_concept.definition: Markovnikov's Rule states that in the addition of a protic acid (HX) to an unsymmetrical alkene, the hydrogen atom bonds to the carbon with more hydrogen substituents, and the halide (X) bonds to the carbon with fewer hydrogen substituents (the more substituted carbon).
key_concept.context: This rule applies to electrophilic addition reactions of alkenes with HX (HCl, HBr, HI), hydration reactions (H₂O/H⁺), and other reactions that proceed through carbocation intermediates. The rule is based on carbocation stability: tertiary (3°) > secondary (2°) > primary (1°) > methyl.

image.alt: Visual representation of Markovnikov's Rule with propene example
image.caption: Markovnikov's Rule: H adds to CH₂ (more H's), Br adds to CH (more substituted) in propene + HBr.

principles.statement: The regioselectivity of alkene addition reactions is determined by the relative stability of the carbocation intermediates that can form during the reaction.
principles.application: When HX adds to an unsymmetrical alkene, the proton (H⁺) can add to either carbon of the double bond, forming two possible carbocations. The more stable carbocation forms faster (lower activation energy) and leads to the major product. This explains why the halogen ends up on the more substituted carbon.
principles.analogy: Think of carbocations like people in a crowd: a tertiary carbocation has three carbon 'friends' to help support it (donate electron density), making it more stable. A primary carbocation has only one 'friend', making it less stable and less likely to form. The reaction takes the 'easier path' through the more stable intermediate.
principles.example: Propene + HBr: Adding H⁺ to C1 forms a secondary carbocation (more stable); adding H⁺ to C2 forms a primary carbocation (less stable). The secondary carbocation pathway dominates, giving 2-bromopropane as the major product (not 1-bromopropane).

principles.principle: Hyperconjugation and Inductive Effects Stabilize Carbocations
principles.statement: Carbocation stability increases with the number of alkyl groups attached due to hyperconjugation (σ-bond electron donation) and inductive effects (electron donation through σ-bonds).
principles.application: Each adjacent C-H or C-C bond can overlap with the empty p-orbital of the carbocation, delocalizing the positive charge and stabilizing the intermediate. This is why 3° > 2° > 1° > methyl in stability. Understanding this explains why Markovnikov's rule works at the electronic level.
principles.analogy: Imagine the carbocation as a person carrying a heavy burden (positive charge). Alkyl groups are like friends who help carry the burden. More friends (alkyl groups) = easier to carry = more stable. A tertiary carbocation has three friends; a primary has only one.
principles.example: Tertiary carbocation: 9 hyperconjugative interactions (from 3 methyl groups). Secondary carbocation: 6 hyperconjugative interactions. Primary carbocation: 3 hyperconjugative interactions. More interactions = greater stability.

components.definition: Markovnikov addition involves three key components: (1) the unsymmetrical alkene substrate with different substitution patterns on each carbon, (2) the protic acid reagent (HX) that provides H⁺ and X⁻, and (3) the carbocation intermediate that determines regioselectivity.
components.interpretation: The rule predicts that X will attach to the carbon that can best stabilize positive charge (more substituted). This is not about the final product stability but about the intermediate carbocation stability during the reaction pathway.

image.alt: Diagram showing carbocation stability order with hyperconjugation
image.caption: Carbocation stability: 3° > 2° > 1° > methyl due to hyperconjugation and inductive effects.

sections.introduction: Understanding the step-by-step mechanism of Markovnikov addition explains why the rule works. The reaction proceeds through a two-step electrophilic addition mechanism with a carbocation intermediate. The first step (protonation) is rate-determining and determines regioselectivity; the second step (nucleophilic attack) is fast and does not affect product distribution.

process_flow.description: The π-electrons of the alkene attack the proton (H⁺) from HX, forming a carbocation intermediate
process_flow.mechanism: The alkene π-bond acts as a nucleophile, attacking the electrophilic H⁺. Two possible carbocations can form depending on which carbon receives the proton. The more stable carbocation forms preferentially due to lower activation energy.
process_flow.result: Carbocation intermediate formed; X⁻ ion released; this step determines regioselectivity
applications: Rate-determining step controls overall reaction rate

process_flow.description: The halide ion (X⁻) attacks the carbocation, forming the final alkyl halide product
process_flow.mechanism: The halide ion (nucleophile) attacks the electron-deficient carbocation carbon, forming a new C-X σ-bond. This step is fast and does not affect regioselectivity since the carbocation is already formed.
process_flow.result: Alkyl halide product formed; Markovnikov product predominates
applications: Does not affect regioselectivity (carbocation already determined)

worked_examples.title: Mechanism for Propene + HBr
worked_examples.problem: Draw the complete mechanism for the reaction of propene with HBr, showing both possible carbocation pathways and explaining why 2-bromopropane is the major product.

step_by_step.explanation: Draw the alkene structure and identify the two possible protonation sites.
Show Pathway A: H⁺ adds to C1, forming secondary carbocation.
Show Pathway B: H⁺ adds to C2, forming primary carbocation.
Compare carbocation stability and determine major pathway.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Secondary > Primary stability} \rightarrow \text{Pathway A dominates} \rightarrow \text{2-bromopropane is major product}$

step_by_step.explanation: Show Br⁻ attack on secondary carbocation to form product.

worked_examples.final_answer: Major product: 2-bromopropane (via secondary carbocation). Minor product: 1-bromopropane (via primary carbocation). The secondary carbocation is more stable due to greater hyperconjugation (6 interactions vs. 3), so Pathway A has lower activation energy and dominates.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Markovnikov addition mechanism: protonation forms carbocation (rate-determining, regioselectivity-determining); halide attack is fast. Carbocation stability (2° > 1°) explains product distribution.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Product for 2-Methylpropene + HCl
worked_examples.problem: Predict the major product when 2-methylpropene reacts with HCl. Draw the mechanism and explain using carbocation stability.

step_by_step.explanation: Draw the alkene structure.
Identify possible carbocations from protonation.
Compare stability: tertiary vs. primary carbocation.
Form product from more stable carbocation.

worked_examples.final_answer: Major product: 2-chloro-2-methylpropane (tert-butyl chloride). The tertiary carbocation is vastly more stable than primary (9 vs. 3 hyperconjugative interactions), so >99% of product forms via the tertiary pathway. This is a classic example of Markovnikov selectivity.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Tertiary carbocations are much more stable than primary due to hyperconjugation and inductive effects. Large stability differences lead to high regioselectivity in Markovnikov additions.

sections.section_title: Carbocation Stability: The Foundation of Markovnikov's Rule
sections.introduction: Carbocation stability is the key to understanding why Markovnikov's Rule works. The stability order (3° > 2° > 1° > methyl) arises from electronic effects that stabilize the electron-deficient carbocation center. Understanding these effects allows prediction of regioselectivity beyond simple memorization of the rule.

categories.description: Overlap of adjacent C-H or C-C σ-bonds with the empty p-orbital of the carbocation
categories.detailed_mechanism: Each adjacent C-H bond can donate electron density into the empty p-orbital of the carbocation through hyperconjugation. This delocalizes the positive charge and stabilizes the intermediate. More adjacent C-H bonds = more hyperconjugation = greater stability. Tertiary carbocations have 9 hyperconjugative interactions; secondary have 6; primary have 3; methyl has 0.

examples.explanation: Three alkyl groups provide 9 C-H bonds for hyperconjugation. Most stable carbocation type. Forms readily in Markovnikov additions to alkenes with tertiary carbon possibilities.

image.alt: Diagram showing hyperconjugation in tertiary carbocation
image.caption: Tertiary carbocation: 9 hyperconjugative interactions from three methyl groups.

examples.explanation: Only one alkyl group provides 3 C-H bonds for hyperconjugation. Least stable (except methyl). Rarely forms in solution; reactions avoid primary carbocation pathways when possible.

categories.description: Electron donation through σ-bonds from alkyl groups to the electron-deficient carbocation center
categories.detailed_mechanism: Alkyl groups are electron-donating through the inductive effect (+I effect). They push electron density through σ-bonds toward the positively charged carbon, partially neutralizing the charge and stabilizing the carbocation. More alkyl groups = greater inductive stabilization. This effect operates through bonds (not space) and decreases with distance.

examples.explanation: Each alkyl group donates electron density through σ-bonds. Three alkyl groups (tertiary) provide maximum inductive stabilization; one alkyl group (primary) provides minimal stabilization.

image.alt: Diagram showing inductive electron donation to carbocation
image.caption: Inductive effect: alkyl groups donate electron density through σ-bonds to stabilize positive charge.

categories.description: Carbocations adjacent to π-systems (allylic, benzylic) are stabilized by resonance delocalization
categories.detailed_mechanism: When a carbocation is adjacent to a double bond (allylic) or aromatic ring (benzylic), the positive charge can be delocalized through resonance. This provides additional stabilization beyond hyperconjugation and inductive effects. Allylic and benzylic carbocations are more stable than typical secondary carbocations, affecting Markovnikov selectivity in conjugated systems.

examples.explanation: Positive charge delocalized over two carbons via resonance. More stable than typical secondary carbocation. Explains regioselectivity in conjugated diene additions.
Positive charge delocalized into aromatic ring via resonance. Very stable (similar to tertiary). Explains high reactivity of benzylic positions in substitution and addition reactions.

image.caption: Benzylic carbocation: positive charge delocalized into aromatic ring via resonance.

sections.key_concept: Carbocation stability order: 3° > 2° > 1° > methyl (hyperconjugation + inductive). Allylic and benzylic carbocations are additionally stabilized by resonance. Stability determines which carbocation forms preferentially, which determines Markovnikov product.

sections.introduction: Applying Markovnikov's Rule systematically ensures correct product prediction for any alkene addition reaction. This section provides a step-by-step approach that works for all electrophilic addition reactions proceeding through carbocation intermediates.

components.composition: Locate the C=C double bond and count hydrogens on each carbon
components.function: Determines which carbon has more vs. fewer hydrogens
components.key_feature: Markovnikov's Rule only applies to unsymmetrical alkenes; symmetrical alkenes give single products regardless

components.composition: Confirm reagent is HX (HCl, HBr, HI) or H₂O/H⁺ (hydration)
components.key_feature: Rule applies to protic acid additions; does not apply to radical additions (anti-Markovnikov) or concerted additions (no carbocation)

components.composition: Draw both possible carbocation intermediates from protonation
components.function: Identifies which pathway is more favorable
components.key_feature: Compare carbocation stability: 3° > 2° > 1°; consider resonance stabilization for allylic/benzylic positions

components.composition: Choose the pathway through the more stable carbocation intermediate
components.key_feature: More stable carbocation forms faster (lower Eₐ); leads to major product

components.composition: Attach X to the carbocation carbon to form final product
components.key_feature: X ends up on more substituted carbon (Markovnikov product); H ends up on less substituted carbon

sections.section_title: Anti-Markovnikov Addition: Exceptions to the Rule
sections.introduction: While Markovnikov's Rule predicts the major product for most electrophilic additions, certain conditions lead to anti-Markovnikov products where the halogen adds to the less substituted carbon. Understanding these exceptions is crucial for synthetic planning and demonstrates that the rule is based on mechanism, not an absolute law.

principles.principle: Radical Addition of HBr Gives Anti-Markovnikov Products
principles.statement: In the presence of peroxides (ROOR) or light, HBr adds to alkenes via a radical mechanism that gives anti-Markovnikov regioselectivity.
principles.application: This is the Kharasch effect (peroxide effect). The mechanism involves bromine radicals (Br•) rather than carbocations. Br• adds first to form the more stable carbon radical (opposite of carbocation stability), then H adds. This gives anti-Markovnikov products. Note: Only HBr shows this effect; HCl and HI do not.
principles.analogy: Think of it as two different 'teams' playing by different rules: the ionic team (no peroxides) follows Markovnikov via carbocations; the radical team (with peroxides) follows anti-Markovnikov via radicals. Different mechanisms = different products.
principles.example: Propene + HBr (no peroxides) → 2-bromopropane (Markovnikov). Propene + HBr + peroxides → 1-bromopropane (anti-Markovnikov). The presence of peroxides completely reverses regioselectivity.

principles.statement: Hydroboration-oxidation (BH₃ then H₂O₂/OH⁻) converts alkenes to alcohols with anti-Markovnikov regioselectivity and syn stereochemistry.
principles.application: This reaction proceeds through a concerted four-membered transition state (no carbocation), so Markovnikov's Rule does not apply. Boron adds to the less substituted carbon (steric control), then oxidation replaces B with OH. This is the primary method for synthesizing primary alcohols from terminal alkenes.
principles.analogy: Hydroboration is like a 'bypass' around the carbocation 'toll road': it takes a different route (concerted mechanism) that avoids the carbocation intermediate entirely, so Markovnikov's Rule (which is based on carbocation stability) doesn't apply.
principles.example: 1-Hexene + BH₃ then H₂O₂/OH⁻ → 1-hexanol (anti-Markovnikov, primary alcohol). 1-Hexene + H₂O/H⁺ → 2-hexanol (Markovnikov, secondary alcohol). Different methods give different regioisomers.

worked_examples.title: Comparing Markovnikov vs. Anti-Markovnikov Addition
worked_examples.problem: Predict the major products when 1-methylcyclohexene reacts with: (a) HBr, (b) HBr + peroxides, (c) BH₃ then H₂O₂/OH⁻. Explain the difference in regioselectivity.

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze reaction (a): HBr addition (ionic mechanism).
Predict product (a): Br adds to more substituted carbon.
Analyze reaction (b): HBr + peroxides (radical mechanism).
Predict product (b): Br adds to less substituted carbon.
Analyze reaction (c): hydroboration-oxidation (concerted mechanism).
Predict product (c): OH adds to less substituted carbon.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) 1-Bromo-1-methylcyclohexane (Markovnikov, ionic); (b) 1-bromo-2-methylcyclohexane (anti-Markovnikov, radical); (c) trans-2-methylcyclohexanol (anti-Markovnikov alcohol, concerted). Same alkene gives three different products depending on reaction conditions and mechanism.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Regioselectivity depends on mechanism: ionic (carbocation) = Markovnikov; radical = anti-Markovnikov (HBr only); hydroboration = anti-Markovnikov alcohol. Mechanism determines product.

sections.introduction: Carbocation intermediates can undergo rearrangements (hydride or alkyl shifts) to form more stable carbocations before the halide attacks. This can lead to products that appear to violate Markovnikov's Rule but actually reflect the rearranged carbocation structure. Recognizing rearrangement possibilities is essential for accurate product prediction.

categories.description: Migration of a hydrogen atom with its bonding electrons from an adjacent carbon to the carbocation
categories.detailed_mechanism: A hydride (H:⁻) shifts from an adjacent carbon to the carbocation center, moving the positive charge to the adjacent carbon. This occurs when the new carbocation is more stable (e.g., 2° → 3°). The shift is fast and occurs before halide attack, so the final product reflects the rearranged carbocation structure.

examples.explanation: Secondary carbocation rearranges to tertiary via hydride shift. Product structure differs from 'expected' Markovnikov product without rearrangement. Common in additions to alkenes with adjacent tertiary carbons.

image.caption: Hydride shift: 2° carbocation rearranges to more stable 3° carbocation before halide attack.

categories.description: Migration of an alkyl group (with bonding electrons) from an adjacent carbon to the carbocation
categories.detailed_mechanism: An alkyl group (methyl, ethyl, etc.) shifts from an adjacent carbon to the carbocation center, moving the positive charge. This occurs when the new carbocation is more stable. Alkyl shifts are less common than hydride shifts but important when no hydride is available for migration.

examples.explanation: Methyl group migrates to carbocation center, forming more stable carbocation. Product has different carbon skeleton than starting alkene. Important in terpene and steroid chemistry.

sections.key_concept: Carbocation rearrangements (hydride or alkyl shifts) occur when a more stable carbocation is accessible. Products reflect the rearranged carbocation structure, not the initial carbocation. Always check for rearrangement possibilities when predicting Markovnikov addition products.

sections.section_title: Common Mistakes in Applying Markovnikov's Rule
sections.introduction: Students often make predictable errors when applying Markovnikov's Rule. Understanding these pitfalls helps ensure accurate product prediction and deeper mechanistic understanding.

categories.type: Mistake: Memorizing Rule Without Understanding Mechanism
categories.description: Applying 'H adds to carbon with more H's' without considering carbocation stability
categories.detailed_mechanism: Students memorize the rule as a phrase without understanding it arises from carbocation stability. This leads to errors when the rule doesn't apply (radical additions, hydroboration) or when rearrangements occur. Understanding the mechanism (carbocation stability) is more reliable than memorizing the phrase.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: 'H always adds to carbon with more H's' (applies to all reactions). CORRECT: 'H adds to form more stable carbocation' (applies to ionic additions only). Mechanism-based thinking handles exceptions and rearrangements correctly.

categories.description: Predicting products without checking for possible hydride or alkyl shifts
categories.detailed_mechanism: Students often draw the 'expected' Markovnikov product without considering that the carbocation may rearrange to a more stable form before halide attack. This leads to incorrect product structures, especially with alkenes that can form tertiary carbocations via rearrangement.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: 3-chloro-2,2-dimethylbutane (no rearrangement). CORRECT: 2-chloro-2,3-dimethylbutane (methyl shift to form tertiary carbocation). Always check if carbocation can rearrange to more stable form.
examples.equation: $\text{Check for rearrangement before drawing final product}$

categories.type: Mistake: Applying Rule to Radical Additions
categories.description: Using Markovnikov's Rule for HBr + peroxides reactions
categories.detailed_mechanism: Markovnikov's Rule applies only to ionic (carbocation) mechanisms. Radical additions of HBr (with peroxides) give anti-Markovnikov products. Students often miss the peroxide condition and apply Markovnikov incorrectly.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: 2-bromopropane (Markovnikov). CORRECT: 1-bromopropane (anti-Markovnikov, radical mechanism). Peroxides change the mechanism from ionic to radical, reversing regioselectivity.

categories.type: Mistake: Confusing Symmetrical and Unsymmetrical Alkenes
categories.description: Applying Markovnikov's Rule to symmetrical alkenes where it's irrelevant
categories.detailed_mechanism: Markovnikov's Rule only matters for unsymmetrical alkenes where two different products are possible. For symmetrical alkenes (e.g., ethene, 2-butene), only one product forms regardless of which carbon receives H vs. X. Students sometimes overthink symmetrical cases.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: 'Which carbon gets Br?' (both carbons are equivalent). CORRECT: Only one product (2-bromobutane) forms because the alkene is symmetrical. Markovnikov's Rule is irrelevant for symmetrical alkenes.

categories.type: Mistake: Miscounting Hydrogens on Alkene Carbons
categories.description: Incorrectly counting hydrogens on alkene carbons, leading to wrong regioselectivity prediction
categories.detailed_mechanism: In line-angle diagrams, hydrogens on carbons are implicit. Students sometimes miscount hydrogens on alkene carbons, leading to incorrect application of the rule. Always explicitly count hydrogens on each alkene carbon before applying the rule.

examples.explanation: Structure: CH₃-CH=C(CH₃)₂. INCORRECT: 'Both carbons have same number of H's.' CORRECT: Left carbon has 1 H; right carbon has 0 H's. H adds to left carbon; Br adds to right carbon (tertiary).
examples.equation: $\text{Explicitly count H's on each alkene carbon before applying rule}$

sections.introduction: Apply your understanding of Markovnikov's Rule to these practice problems. Work through product prediction, mechanism drawing, and rearrangement identification to build proficiency.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Major Products for Multiple Alkenes
worked_examples.problem: Predict the major product for each reaction: (a) 2-Methyl-2-butene + HCl, (b) 1-Pentene + HBr, (c) 3,3-Dimethyl-1-butene + HCl (consider rearrangement).

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze reaction (a): 2-methyl-2-butene + HCl.
Predict product (a): Cl adds to tertiary carbon.
Analyze reaction (b): 1-pentene + HBr.
Predict product (b): Br adds to secondary carbon.
Analyze reaction (c): 3,3-dimethyl-1-butene + HCl with rearrangement.
Predict product (c): Cl adds to rearranged tertiary carbon.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) 2-Chloro-2-methylbutane (tertiary chloride, Markovnikov); (b) 2-Bromopentane (secondary bromide, Markovnikov); (c) 2-Chloro-2,3-dimethylbutane (rearranged product via methyl shift). Always check for rearrangements when carbocations form.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Markovnikov addition: halogen adds to more substituted carbon via more stable carbocation. Check for rearrangements (hydride or alkyl shifts) that can change product structure.

worked_examples.title: Distinguishing Markovnikov from Anti-Markovnikov Conditions
worked_examples.problem: For 1-methylcyclohexene, predict products with: (a) HBr, (b) HBr + peroxides, (c) H₂O/H₂SO₄. Explain the difference.

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze reaction (a): HBr (ionic mechanism).
Predict product (a): Br adds to tertiary carbon.
Analyze reaction (b): HBr + peroxides (radical mechanism).
Predict product (b): Br adds to secondary carbon.
Analyze reaction (c): H₂O/H₂SO₄ (hydration, ionic mechanism).
Predict product (c): OH adds to tertiary carbon.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) 1-Bromo-1-methylcyclohexane (Markovnikov, ionic); (b) 1-bromo-2-methylcyclohexane (anti-Markovnikov, radical); (c) 1-methylcyclohexanol (Markovnikov alcohol, ionic). Reaction conditions determine mechanism, which determines regioselectivity.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Mechanism determines regioselectivity: ionic (carbocation) = Markovnikov; radical (HBr + peroxides) = anti-Markovnikov; hydration (H₂O/H⁺) = Markovnikov alcohol.

worked_examples.title: Carbocation Rearrangement Problem
worked_examples.problem: Predict the product when 3-methyl-1-butene reacts with HCl. Show the mechanism including any rearrangements.

step_by_step.explanation: Draw the alkene structure and identify initial carbocation.
Check for possible rearrangement to more stable carbocation.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Adjacent C3 is tertiary} \rightarrow \text{hydride shift possible} \rightarrow \text{tertiary carbocation}$

step_by_step.explanation: Perform hydride shift rearrangement.
Add chloride to rearranged carbocation.

worked_examples.final_answer: Major product: 2-chloro-2-methylbutane (not 2-chloro-3-methylbutane). Carbocation rearrangement occurs: secondary carbocation rearranges to more stable tertiary carbocation via hydride shift before chloride addition.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Carbocation rearrangements in Markovnikov additions: always check if hydride or alkyl shift can produce more stable carbocation. Product reflects rearranged carbocation structure.

sections.introduction: A quick-reference checklist consolidates the key concepts for applying Markovnikov's Rule correctly and avoiding common mistakes.

components.composition: Confirm ionic addition (HX, H₂O/H⁺) not radical (HBr + peroxides) or concerted (hydroboration)
components.key_feature: Markovnikov applies to ionic (carbocation) mechanisms only; radical and concerted mechanisms give anti-Markovnikov

components.composition: Count hydrogens on each alkene carbon
components.function: Determines which carbon is more vs. less substituted
components.key_feature: Rule only matters for unsymmetrical alkenes; symmetrical alkenes give single products

components.composition: Show both carbocation intermediates from protonation
components.function: Identifies which pathway is more favorable
components.key_feature: Compare stability: 3° > 2° > 1°; consider resonance for allylic/benzylic

components.composition: Look for hydride or alkyl shifts to more stable carbocation
components.key_feature: Rearrangement occurs before halide attack; product reflects rearranged carbocation

components.composition: Attach X to carbocation carbon to form final product
components.key_feature: X ends up on more substituted carbon (Markovnikov product)

summary: Markovnikov's Rule predicts regioselectivity in electrophilic addition of protic acids (HX) to unsymmetrical alkenes: H adds to carbon with more H's; X adds to more substituted carbon. The rule arises from carbocation stability (3° > 2° > 1° > methyl) due to hyperconjugation and inductive effects. Mechanism: (1) π-electrons attack H⁺, forming carbocation (rate-determining, regioselectivity-determining); (2) X⁻ attacks carbocation, forming product. Key principles: more stable carbocation forms faster (lower Eₐ); stability order reflects number of alkyl groups donating electron density. Exceptions: (1) HBr + peroxides gives anti-Markovnikov via radical mechanism (Kharasch effect); (2) Hydroboration-oxidation gives anti-Markovnikov alcohols via concerted mechanism. Carbocation rearrangements (hydride or alkyl shifts) occur when more stable carbocation is accessible, changing product structure. Common mistakes: memorizing rule without understanding mechanism, ignoring rearrangements, applying to radical additions, confusing symmetrical/unsymmetrical alkenes, miscounting hydrogens. Mastery requires understanding carbocation stability, recognizing rearrangement possibilities, and identifying reaction conditions that determine mechanism (ionic vs. radical vs. concerted).
